coredata.dc:title: Graphene-quantum-dots induced NiCo2S4 with hierarchical-like hollow nanostructure for supercapacitors with enhanced electrochemical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yuanyuan
Shi, Tielin
Zhong, Yan
Cheng, Siyi
Jiang, Shulan
Chen, Chen
Liao, Guanglan
Tang, Zirong
coredata.dc:description: As a new class of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted increasing attentions due to their unique properties of both graphene and quantum dots. In this paper, GQDs-decorated hierarchical-like hollow NiCo2S4 nanostructures are fabricated through simple two-step hydrothermal reactions. Compared with the bare NiCo2S4 nanowires, it is found that the surface morphology and internal structure have been changed with the introduction of the GQDs during the similar growth process. The unique nanostructures have resulted in better electrochemical performance for supercapacitors, such as good rate capability, high specific capacitance (678.22 F g−1 at the current density of 0.2 A g−1) and enhanced cycling stability (94% retention after 5000 cycles), due to the lower impedance, larger specific surface area, more interspace and pathways for both ion diffusion and volume changes. These results show that the introduction of the GQDs during the fabrication process could be a feasible route to obtain novel nanostructures, and the GQDs decorated nanocomposites are promising for the development of next generation electrochemical devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
NiCo2S4
Supercapacitors
GQDs decorated NiCo2S4 nanocomposites
Hierarchical-like hollow nanostructure

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the NiCo2S4 nanowire arrays

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the two-step synthetic routes for the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites and NiCo2S4 NWAs.
SEM images of the NiCo2S4 NWAs (A and B) grown on Ni foam at different magnifications. SEM images of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites grown on Ni foam at different magnifications (C and D).
(A and B) TEM images of the NiCo2S4 NWAs. (C) TEM image of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites. (D) HRTEM image of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites.
Corresponding electron energy loss spectroscopy elemental mapping of (A) C, (B) Ni, (C) Co and (D) S elements on the hollow GODs/NiCo2S4 nanostructure.
XRD patterns of the NiCo2S4 and GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites.
(A) XPS spectra of NiCo2S4NWAs and GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites, XPS spectra of the (B) S 2p, (C) Ni 2p, (D) Co 2p of the NiCo2S4 and GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites.

(A–D) Morphology evolution process of NiCo2S4 nanowires during the sulfuration process and (E-H) morphology evolution process for the formation of the hierarchical-like hollow GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites: TEM images of the samples synthesize under different sulfuration durations of 0 h, 1 h, 3 h and 6 h.

Electrochemical performance of the NiCo2S4 NWAs and the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrodes: (A) CV curves at the scan rate of 10 mV s−1, (B) CV curves of NiCo2S4@GQDs nanocomposite electrodes at different scan rates, (C) Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrodes at different scan rates, (D) The discharge mass capacitance of NiCo2S4 and GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites at different scan rates, (E) Cycling properties of NiCo2S4 and GQDs/NiCo2S4nanocomposites at the current density of 20 m A cm−2 for 5000 cycles, (F) EIS Nyquist plots.

head.ce:title: Graphene-quantum-dots induced NiCo2S4 with hierarchical-like hollow nanostructure for supercapacitors with enhanced electrochemical performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Digital Manufacturing Equipment and Technology, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Digital Manufacturing Equipment and Technology
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tribology Research Institute, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 610031, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. 1037 Luoyu Road, Wuhan 430074, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new class of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted increasing attentions due to their unique properties of both graphene and quantum dots. In this paper, GQDs-decorated hierarchical-like hollow NiCo2S4 nanostructures are fabricated through simple two-step hydrothermal reactions. Compared with the bare NiCo2S4 nanowires, it is found that the surface morphology and internal structure have been changed with the introduction of the GQDs during the similar growth process. The unique nanostructures have resulted in better electrochemical performance for supercapacitors, such as good rate capability, high specific capacitance (678.22 F g−1 at the current density of 0.2 A g−1) and enhanced cycling stability (94% retention after 5000 cycles), due to the lower impedance, larger specific surface area, more interspace and pathways for both ion diffusion and volume changes. These results show that the introduction of the GQDs during the fabrication process could be a feasible route to obtain novel nanostructures, and the GQDs decorated nanocomposites are promising for the development of next generation electrochemical devices.

ce:section.ce:para: With the fast energy consumption and technology development, the demand for high-performance energy storage devices and technologies is pressing [1]. Supercapacitor is an energy storage device which has attracted vast interests owing to the high charge-discharge rates and long life-times comparing with other energy storage devices [2]. For fabricating high-performance supercapacitor, it is critical to develop favorable electrode materials [3].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are only several nanometers with single or few-layers graphene, have gained increasing popularity in fundamental studies due to their unique properties of both graphene and quantum dots (QDs) [4]. In comparison with the other common QDs, GDQs have the advantages of low toxicity, quick electron mobility, good bio-sensing, tunable bandgaps, strong luminescence and photostability [5]. Hence, GQDs have been studied and applied in multidisciplinary fields such as fuel cells [6], bio-imaging [7], bio-sensing [8], photovoltaics [9] and light-emitting diodes [10]. Most recently, several studies on the electrochemical capacitive properties of GDQs and their applications in electrode materials have been reported, like the GDQs micro-supercapacitors [11], the GDQ/RuO2 supercapacitors [12] and the GDQs-CNT supercapacitors [13]. These studies have shown that the GDQs are promising in enhancing the specific capacitance, the rate performance, and the cycling stability for electrochemical applications. However, these applications in the development of high-performance supercapacitors are still in the early stage, which deserve further investigations.

Numerous transition metal oxides/hydroxides, and correlative compounds have been explored for fabricating high-performance supercapacitors since they have demonstrated high theoretical capacity, low cost, low toxicity, abundant reversible redox reactions, great flexibility in structure and morphology [14]. However, the poor electron transport ability and quick capacity decay at high rates limit their extensive applications. As a new type of energy storage material, transition metal sulfides, such as cobalt sulfides [15] and nickel sulfides [16], have been studied recently, which shown satisfactory electrochemical performance on the high specific capacity and nice electrical conductivity. Among various transition metal sulfide materials, NiCo2S4 has been widely investigated and applied on supercapacitors, solar-cells, and electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction and evolution reactions [17–19]. In contrast with the single-element sulfides, NiCo2S4 contains two different metal cations, which ensures the richer redox reactions and owns higher electronic conductivity. However, the application of NiCo2S4 also performs low stability and low energy density due to the active redox reactions, ineffective contact, and the destruction of structure during the redox reactions. To address shortcomings above, various carbon materials such as graphene [19], active carbon [20], and carbon nanotube [21] have been employed to enhance the electron-transfer ability, the contacting between the active material and electrolyte, along with surface wettability. However, such strategies can solve the above problems partially, with other issues occurring as well [22–24]. For example, a dense carbon coating will hinder the reactions between metal sulfides particles and electrolyte, but the overweight carbon material can decrease the specific capacitance in the whole electrode due to the rather small capacitance of the carbon material themselves. In contrast, 0D GQDs have many natural merits, and their integration in NiCo2S4 structures may overcome as-described shortcomings without generating new problems.

In this work, we adopt a simple two-step hydrothermal method to synthesize the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites. Through adding a spot of GQDs during the fabrication process, unique hierarchal-like hollow GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanostructure unexpectedly obtains. The newly synthesized 3D nanocomposites are applied as supercapacitor electrodes and their electrochemical performance is evaluated by comparing with the bare NiCo2S4 electrode. As-prepared GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode demonstrates a high mass specific capacitance (678.22 F g−1 at the current density of 0.2 A g−1) and good cycling stability (94% retention after 5000 cycles).

HCl, ethanol, CoCl2*6H2O, NiCl2*6H2O, urea, and sodium sulfide were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ni foam (thickness:< 1.7 mm) was provided by Shanxi Taiyuan Lithium Technology Co. Ltd. Graphene quantum dots were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co. Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the NiCo2S4 nanowire arrays
ce:section.ce:para: For synthesis of NiCo2S4, CoCl2*6H2O (8 mmol), NiCl2*6H2O (4 mmol) and urea (12 mmol) were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water, then the as-obtained solution was transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave with a piece of clean Ni foam. After 6 h growth at 120 °C, the precursors grown on the Ni foam were received. Next, the Ni foam with the precursors was added into a 0.16 M sodium sulfide solution and maintain at 160 °C for 6 h. Finally, the samples were collected after being washed with deionized water and ethanol.

In order to synthesize the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites, CoCl2*6H2O (8 mmol), NiCl2*6H2O (4 mmol), urea (12 mmol) and 10 mg GQDs were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water, then as-obtained solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with a piece of clean Ni foam. After 6 h growth at 120 °C, the precursors grown on the Ni foam were received. Afterwards, the Ni foam with the precursors was put into a 0.16 M sodium sulfide solution and maintained at 160 °C for 6 h. Finally, the samples were collected after being washed with deionized water and ethanol. To observe the morphology evolution of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites, samples with different sulfuration durations of 0 h, 1 h, and 3 h were respectively prepared during the second step with the same other conditions.

We also considered the effect of GQDs content in the NiCo2S4 on the capacitance and tune the hydrothermal process temperature in order to optimized the capacitance of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 electrode. The CV cures of the different GQDs/NiCo2S4 electrodes with different GQDs amounts (5, 10, 20 mg) are shown in Fig. S1. The initial result demonstrates that the composite with 10 mg GQDs owns better electrochemical performance, which was employed this work.

The morphology and structure of the samples were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Nova NanoSEM 450), X-ray diffraction (XRD) with radiation from a Cu target (Kα, λ = 0.154 nm), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a FEI Tecnai G2 S-TWIN microscope, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller specific surface area and pore size with the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) with non-monochromatized Mgkα X-ray radiation as the excitation source.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out in three-electrode system using an electrochemical Autolab workstation (PGSTAT-302 N, Eco Echemie B.V. Company). The area of each working electrode was 1 × 1 cm2 with typical electroactive GQDs/NiCo2S4 loading of 15 mg cm−2 and NiCo2S4 loading of 10 mg cm−2. A Pt plate was adopted as the counter electrode and the reference electrode was chosen as a saturated calomel electrode (SCE). 3 M KOH aqueous was used as the electrolyte. The EIS measurements were carried out with 10 mV amplitude in a frequency range from 10−2 Hz–105 Hz at open-circuit potential. Galvanostatic charging/discharging (GCD) and cycling tests were carried out using a battery measurement system (LAND CT2001A). The mass specific capacitance (Cm) and areal specific capacitance (Ca), were calculated based on the following equations [25]:
(1)
C m
= I×t /
( m× Δ V )
(2)
C a
= I×t /
( A× Δ V )
where I, t, m, ΔV and A mean the discharge current (mA), the discharge time (s), the mass of the active materials in the electrodes (g), the potential window (V), and the surface area of the electrode (cm2), respectively.

The synthetic routes for the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites and NiCo2S4 NWAs are shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, Co+ and Ni+ cations react with the hydrolysis products of urea to form the bimetallic carbonate hydroxide GQDs composite precursors (Fig. 1A–B). After the continuous sulfuration process, the precursors evolve into GQDs/NiCo2S4 hierarchical-like nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 1B–C. Simultaneously, the NiCo2S4 NWAs with smooth surface are fabricated through two-step hydrothermal reactions, as presented in Fig. 1A-D-E.

The morphologies of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites and the bare NiCo2S4 NWAs are observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM), as shown in Fig. 2 . As demonstrated in Fig. 2A and B, Ni foam is completely covered by the orderly smooth NiCo2S4 NWAs. Compared with the bare NiCo2S4 NWAs, the morphology of the GQD/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites is different, as shown in Fig. 2C and D. The high magnification SEM image of the GQD/NiCo2S4 reveals a rough surface, which is composed of intimately intertwined branch nanoflakes on the surface. The nanoflakes combine with the core forming the hierarchical-like nanostructure. The formation of such unique structure may be attributed to the GQDs, which will restrain the anisotropy growth of the NiCo2S4 during the sulfuration process. Therefore, the hierarchical-like GQD/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites evolve from the smooth GQDs/Ni-Co precursor (Fig. S2). This morphology evolution can greatly increase the specific surface area and buffer the volume changes in the redox reactions of electrodes for high-performance supercapacitors.

As demonstrated by the N2 sorption measurements (Fig. S4), the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller specific surface area of the hierarchical-like GQDs/NiCo2S4 is ∼35 m2 g−1 (almost twice of that of the bare NiCo2S4, ∼17.35 m2 g−1). Besides, the half pore size distribution in Fig. S4 illustrates that the pores in both the GQDs/NiCo2S4 and the NiCo2S4 are mesopores. These results can demonstrate the mesoporous architecture of the 3D hierarchical-like GQDs/NiCo2S4 composites.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the NiCo2S4 NWAs and GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 5 . Three typical peaks at 44.7°, 52.1° and 76.5° can be successfully identified as crystal planes of the Ni foam. The other four major peaks at 31.5°, 38.3°, 50.4°, and 55.3° can be indexed of the NiCo2S4 phase (JCPDS card No. 20–0782) [26]. By comparing the two XRD images, a weak and broad peak at 25° appears in the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites XRD pattern, indicating the presence of GQDs.

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) is adopted to investigate the surface elemental composition and chemical states of the NiCo2S4 NWAs and the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites. The full XPS spectra in Fig. 6 A confirms the presence of S, Co, Ni, C in the NiCo2S4 NWAs (∼14.02 at. % S, ∼7.97 at. % Co, ∼17.84 at. % Ni, ∼16.61 at. % C) and the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites (∼8.72 at. % S, ∼10.3 at. % Co, ∼11.07 at. % Ni, ∼20.56 at. % C). The atomic ratio of the C in the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites has an obvious increment as compared with that of the NiCo2S4, which further confirms the successful compositing of the GQDs with the NiCo2S4. After adding the GQDs, the atomic ratio of the C increases, while the atomic ratio of the S and Ni decrease. Therefore, the relative atomic ratio of the S and Ni in the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites are lower than those in the NiCo2S4 NWA. The Ni, Co, S atomic ratios in either the NiCo2S4 NWAs or the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites are not exactly 1:2:4. The high atomic ratio of Ni comes from the Ni foam substrate and the high atomic ratio of S is due to the ambiguous sulfur addition during the sulfuration process. The comparison of the S 2p spectrum in the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites and the bare NiCo2S4 is presented in Fig. 6B, showing no apparent difference. The binding energies at 161.3 and 162.1 Ev respectively correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 in the metal sulfide [27], and another one is shake-up satellite. The spectrum of Co 2p for the hybrid can be deconvoluted into two spin-orbit doublets and two shake-up satellites (Fig. 6C), which is similar to that of the bare one. The first doublet at 778.4 and 793.3eV and the second at 779.3 and 794.8 eV could be respectively assigned to Co3+ and Co2+ [28]. Fig. 6D shows the Ni 2p spectrum with similar characteristic for both GQDs/NiCo2S4 and the bare NiCo2S4, which can be best fitted by two spin-orbit doublets (respectively assigned to Ni2+ and Ni3+) and two shake-up satellites. The peak shapes are similar except for some differences appear in the peak intensities for both the Co and Ni XPS spectra, suggesting that the introduction of the GQDs may affect the cationic distribution of the NiCo2S4 in the nanocomposites.
In general, the introduction of the GQDs leads to the evolution of both morphology and structure of the NiCo2S4, which may greatly affect the electrochemical performance.

To study the morphology and nanostructure formation mechanism of the hierarchical-like hollow GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites, a series of samples fabricated with different sulfuration reaction time (0, 1, 3 and 6 h) are prepared. The morphology evolution process is investigated by TEM, as shown in Fig. 7
. Before the sulfuration reaction, the precursors are single nanowires with smooth surface (Fig. 7E). With the sulfuration reaction going on, the precursor without GQDs is converted into NiCo2S4 nanowires with a slight morphology change (Fig. 7A–D). However, the morphology of the sample with GQDs are greatly changed during the sulfuration process. For the first 1 h sulfuration reaction as shown in Fig. 7F, the structure of the intermediate granulates, which forms a rough surface. After 3 h sulfuration reaction, a thin layer nanosheets forms on the surface, as shown in Fig. 7G. With the reaction going on, the surface thin layer keeps growing and the middle area of the nanocomposite evolves into hollow. In the end, the hierarchical-like hollow nanostructure gradually forms and the final GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites are obtained after 6 h.

Based on the results above, the formation mechanism of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite from its nanowire precursor is proposed as follows. During the sulfuration process, S2− reacted with the GQDs/Ni/Co precursor to form NiCo2S4 nanoparticles on the surface initially. When the surface NiCo2S4 nanoparticles formed in the hydrothermal system, the GQDs locating on the surface of Ni/Co precursor would combine with the newly formed NiCo2S4 nanoparticles due to the coordination effects. Besides, the GQDs would promote the prevention on the anisotropic growth of NiCo2S4 nanoparticles [29], which finally resulted in forming a thin layer of NiCo2S4 nanosheets shell around the surface area. On the other hand, according to the Kirkendall effect, the different diffusivities of various species in a diffusion couple may lead to the formation of Kirkendall voids close to the interface [30]. Herein, the solid precursor reacted with S2−, forming a thin layer of NiCo2S4 shell at the early reaction stage. The direct evolution of the precursor to the NiCo2S4 nanoparticles was obstructed by the thin layer nanosheets shell. Further reaction would continue by the diffusion of S2− ions through the interface. As the diffusion rates of the Ni and Co ions were faster than sulfur ions through the NiCo2S4 shell, the unequal diffusion of reacting species would produce more voids close to the interface. With the reactions continuing, the NiCo2S4 shell increased, the precursor core decreased gradually and the voids gathered together in the middle area of the nanocomposites. Finally, the hollow nanostructure was obtained as well as the precursor nanowire turned into hierarchical-like hollow GQDs/NiCo2S4@NiCo2S4 nanocomposites.

To study the electrochemical performance of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites and NiCo2S4 NWAs, CV curves of the electrodes are investigated in a three-electrode test system with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 over a potential range from −0.2–0.6 V and the results are shown in Fig. 8A. The redox peaks in each CV curve are clearly observed for the electrochemical feature of the NiCo2S4, which correspond to the reversible reactions of Co3+/Co4+, Co2+/Co3+ and Ni2+/Ni3+ transitions as shown in the following equations [17].
(3) CoS + OH− ↔ CoSOH + e−
(4) CoSOH + OH− ↔ CoSO + H2O + e−
(5) NiS + OH− ↔ NiSOH + e−

Compared with the bare NiCo2S4 NWAs electrode, the CV curve peaks are shifted with increased magnitude for that of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode, indicating that the electrochemical performance is improved after adding the GODs in Ref. [31]. In addition, the CV area significantly increases, indicating that GQDs/NiCo2S4 electrodes have larger specific capacitance than that of the bare one, which should be attributed to the introduction of GQDs and the unique hierarchical-like hollow nanostructure. Fig. 8B shows the CV curves of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode at different scan rates. Clearly, with the increase of the scan rates, both the voltammetric current and the anodic peak current increases, the cathodic peak current decreases. This may be explained by the diffusion of hydroxyl ions into the hollow nanostructure. The lower the scan rate, the slower movement of OH− ions. With increasing the scan rate, the movement of the OH− ions becomes faster and the utilization ratio of the electrode material rises, which enrichs the redox reactions [32].

Galvanostatic charge-discharge tests are further performed in a 3 M KOH aqueous solution with the voltage range of 0–0.46 V at different current densities, and the results are shown in Fig. 8C. The capacitance of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode and the bare NiCo2S4 NWAs electrode is estimated. The discharge mass capacitance is calculated using equation (1), as plotted in Fig. 8D. The discharge area capacitance is calculated using equation (2), and results are also provided in Fig. S5. Notably, the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode delivers an average mass specific capacitance of 678.22 ± 32 F g−1 at the current density of 0.2 A g−1 (standard deviation for 3 samples), which is higher than that of the bare NiCo2S4 NWAs electrode (562 ± 5.7 F g−1). With increasing current density, the capacitance slightly decreases, because the electrochemical reaction rate is much slower than the rate of electron transfers, and the electro materials cannot fully participate in the redox reaction. The mass loading of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite is 15 mg cm−2, which is much higher than that of the bare NiCo2S4 NWAs (10 mg cm−2), resulting that the capacitance of the composite decreases rapidly at high current density in comparison to the bare one. However, the capacitance of the composite still maintains at 534 ± 14F g−1 (85.22% retention) when the current increases from 0.2 to 1 A g−1, which still delivers a higher capacitance than that of the bare one (510 ± 42F g−1). The specific capacitance is also higher than some previously reported results of electrodes based on NiCo2S4, such as the NiCo2S4 hollow hexagonal nanoplates (437 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) [33], the NiCo2S4 nano-aggregates (592 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, 2.5–3.5 mg cm−2) [34], and the Ni0.24Co2.58S4 microflowers (379 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1) [35]. The improved specific capacitance of the composited electrode can be attributed to the unique 3D hierarchical-like hollow structure and the well-combination of GQDs with NiCo2S4. It is believed that the 3D hierarchical-like hollow nanostructure offers a larger specific surface area that can improve the contact between electrode and electrolyte. The combination of NiCo2S4 and GQDs with different redox reaction potentials creates more electro active sites for the Faradaic redox reactions.

In addition, the cycling ability is evaluated by repeating the charging-discharging measurement at the constant current density of 20 mA cm−2, as shown in Fig. 8E. During the initial 1000 cycles, the specific capacitance of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode increases gradually, which can be attributed to the complete activation of the electrode. After 5000 cycles, its retention rate is 94%, which is much higher than that of the bare NiCo2S4 electrode (70% after 5000 cycles). The result is also much better than those of previously reported NiCo2S4 based electrodes, such as NiCo2S4 nanosheets grown on Nitrogen-doped carbon foam (9.6% capacity loss after 2000 cycles) [36], NiCo2S4 nanotube@Ni-Mn (11.7% capacity loss after 1000 cycles) [37], additive-free FeCo2S4-NiCo2S4 electrode (85.1% capacitance retention at 40 mA cm−2) [38]. The morphologies of the bare NiCo2S4 NWAs electrode and the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites electrode, after cycling tests, are observed by SEM, as supported in Figs. S6 and S7. After 5000 cycles, the smooth and solid NiCo2S4 NWAs are broken and appear abundant cavities in the center, which may be explained to the intumescent of the nanowires during the long-time cycles (Fig. S6). The mechanism of the appearance of the cavities during the cycles test requires further study. The morphology of the GQD/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode appears to be unchanged and the hierarchical-like nanostructure maintains well after the cycling test, seeing from Fig. S7. Besides, no deformation of the nanoflakes or peeling off from the Ni foam is observed in the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode, indicating a better mechanical stability of the hierarchical-like hollow GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode than the bare NiCo2S4 NWAs electrode. The improved cycling stability can also be ascribed to both the hierarchical-like hollow structure and the introduction of GQDs. Firstly, the thin NiCo2S4 nanoflakes that are connected with each other forming a stable and strong network as the shell, which helps to relieve the structure destroy caused by the volume expansion during cycling process. Secondly, the addition of GQDs can suppress the dissolution and agglomeration of the NiCo2S4 [39]. Thirdly, such the hollow structure of the nanocomposites is porous, which can relax the volume expansion and alleviates the structure damage.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is further conducted on the electrodes at an ac perturbation of 5 mV in the frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with an open circuit potential, as shown in Fig. 8F. The EIS Nyquist plots are fitted by an equivalent circuit, which is composed of an internal resistance Rs, an interfacial charge transfers resistance Rct, a Warburg resistance W, and the double layer capacitance C. Rs for the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode is measured to be 0.29 Ω which is much smaller than that of the bare one (0.37 Ω). GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode also shows a lower Rct (0.012 Ω) than that of the bare one (0.023 Ω). These results manifest that GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode has lower resistance and faster electron-transfer, which can be attributed to the high conductivity of GQDs and the unique 3D hierarchal hollow nanostructure.

In comparison with the bare NiCo2S4 electrode, the improved electrochemical performance of the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode could be related to the unique hierarchical-like hollow structure and the introduction of GQDs, which are discussed in summary, as follows. Firstly, the 3D hierarchical-like hollow structure possess large surface area and mesoporous architecture, which increase the contacting area of the electrode/electrolyte to enable the effective contact of the active materials with the electrolyte and provide more active sites for electrochemical reactions. Therefore, the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode can achieve the maximum harvest of capacitance. In the meantime, the thin NiCo2S4 nanosheets connect with each other forming a mechanical-enhanced shell. Such the hollow nanostructure is beneficial for relaxing the volume expansion and alleviating the structure damage during cycling process. Such a stable and inter-connecting structure achieves an enhanced cycling ability. Secondly, GQDs have tiny size of only several nanometers, which can carry many organic groups for surface wettability, form large interfacial areas among GQDs, NiCo2S4 and electrolytes. GQDs can also accerlerate the ion diffusion and charge transfer kinetics to promote the whole redox reactions completely. In consequence, the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite electrode achieves higher harvest of capacitance. Most importantly, GQDs can also suppress the dissolution and agglomeration of the NiCo2S4 to retain the structure stability.

In summary, the introduction of GQDs can affect the structure formation of NiCo2S4, which could convert solid nanowire into 3D hierarchical-like hollow nanostructure. The GQDs/NiCo2S4 composite electrode shows fast ion diffusions, low electron-transfer resistance, good rate capability, high specific capacitance and enhanced cycling ability. It exhibits high specific capacitance (678.22 F g−1 at 0.2 A g−1), and remarkable cycling stability (94% retention after 5000 cycles). These results show that the GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposites hold great promise as advanced electrode materials for high-performance supercapacitors. We anticipate that the introduction of GQDs is an effective and feasible approach to fabricate smart nanostructure for broad applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Science Foundation of China (nos.51275195), the National Basic Research Program of China (No. 2015CB057205), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and the Innovative Research Team in University (grant no. IRT13017). We would like to thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
mmc1 mmc1

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.02.145.
